column2: At low resolution in whole-slide images, normal lymph nodes appear as well-defined, round to oval structures with a pale, reticular texture due to the interlacing network of collagen fibers and blood vessels. They typically have a smooth, uniform capsule with no irregularities or calcifications. At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a central area of medullary tissue composed of a loose arrangement of reticular cells and macrophages, interspersed with a network of sinusoids. The paracortex region, which is less prominent, consists of a mantle of lymphoid follicles and germinal centers. The stroma is characterized by a fine, delicate reticulin network and a sparse distribution of fibroblasts and immune cells. Overall, normal lymph nodes exhibit a homogeneous, non-heterogeneous appearance without any areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypia.

At low resolution within a whole-slide image, normal lymph nodes appear as well-defined, round to oval structures with a pale, reticular texture due to the interlacing network of collagen fibers and blood vessels. They typically have a smooth, uniform grayish-pink color and contain a central, pale, and slightly darker region representing the medullary and paracortex areas, respectively. At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a clear demarcation between the cortex and medulla. The cortex is composed of a mantle zone surrounding a germinal center, while the medulla contains a network of trabeculae and sinuses. Normal lymph nodes also exhibit a consistent distribution of lymphoid tissue without any areas of necrosis or atypia, and their capsule is intact and clearly delineated from the surrounding stroma.

At low resolution within a whole-slide image, normal lymph nodes appear as well-defined, round to oval structures with a pale, reticular texture due to the arrangement of reticular fibers and stromal cells. They typically have a smooth, uniform capsule that is clearly delineated from the surrounding tissue. At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a fine network of interstitial spaces filled with a loose, fibrovascular stroma, which contains a variable number of lymphoid follicles, germinal centers, and scattered lymphocytes. The sinusoids are lined by a single layer of endothelial cells, and there may be occasional macrophages or histiocytes present. The overall appearance is homogeneous and lacks any areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypia that might suggest malignancy.

Overall, normal lymph nodes exhibit a homogeneous, non-heterogeneous appearance without any areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypical cellular changes.

At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a central area of trabecular and medullary regions, with the trabeculae composed of delicate, branching collagen fibers that form a lattice-like pattern. The medulla contains a mixture of lymphoid and non-lymphoid cells, including macrophages, plasma cells, and occasional lymphocytes, which contribute to a slightly darker, more granular appearance compared to the surrounding stroma. The sinusoids, which are the small blood-filled spaces between the trabeculae, can be observed as fine, dark lines. Overall, normal lymph nodes exhibit a homogeneous, well-organized structure without any areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypia.

At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a central area of medullary tissue containing a meshwork of reticular fibers supporting a population of lymphoid cells, including a mixture of B and T lymphocytes, plasma cells, and occasional macrophages. The paracortex region, which is the outer part of the lymph node, is characterized by a more densely packed arrangement of follicles and germinal centers, with a more pronounced stromal component. The overall structure maintains a balanced and harmonious appearance without any areas of necrosis or atypia that might suggest malignancy.

At higher resolution, the architecture becomes more apparent, showing a clear demarcation between the cortex and medulla. Normal lymph nodes also exhibit a consistent, non-homogeneous texture with occasional small follicles and germinal centers, and no evidence of atypia or abnormal cell clusters that might suggest malignancy.
